text_content:
# MAIN ARTICLE


# White Peroxidase-Mimicking Nanozymes: Colorimetric Pesticide Assay without Interferences of O2and Color


Xin Liang and Lei Han*

Given the promising prospect of peroxidase-like nanozymes in colorimetric assay, it is interesting and significant to eliminate their oxidase-like activities and color interference for nanozyme-based colorimetric assay. Here, the enzyme-mimicking activity of GeO2nanomaterials is discovered. GeO2nanozymes exclusively possess peroxidase-like activity without oxidase-like characteristics, freeing the corresponding assay system from O2interference. Moreover, GeO2nanozymes are white, thereby eliminating the color interference of the nanozymes themselves in nanozymes-based colorimetric assay. Interestingly, compared with other metal oxides, GeO2nanozymes are easily decomposed by thiocholine, acetylcholinesterase (AChE)catalyzed hydrolysate, which triggers an ultrasensitive and anti-interference organophosphate pesticides assay process based on the irreversible inhibition of AChE. The proposed strategy about finding nanozymes with single activity without interference color will open avenues for improving the performance of colorimetric analysis systems based on nanozymes.


# 1. Introduction


As an emerging type of nanomaterial with enzyme-mimicking activity,  nanozymes  have  attracted  extensive  interest  because of  their  high  activity  and  low  cost,  compared  with  natural enzymes. [1-3]   Numerous  studies  have  demonstrated  that  the unique  properties  of  nanozymes  can  be  developed  for  use  in distinct applications. [4,5] Therefore, novel nanozymes with unique properties should be discovered to develop the exclusive, tailor-made applications. As the main subfamily of nanozymes, peroxidase-mimicking  nanozymes  have  been  widely  used  in colorimetric assay. [1-3] Significantly,  many  nanozymes  with peroxidase-like activity also exhibit oxidase-like activity, such as V2O5, [6] MnO2, [7] Co3O4 , [8] CeO2, [9] CuO, [10] Au, [11] Pd, [12] and Pt. [13] Multi-enzyme activity has good applications in many fields. [14,15] Unfortunately,  for  peroxidase-like  nanozymes-based  assay,  the oxidase-like  activity  can  distort  the  results,  because  the  substrates  can  be  directly  oxidized  to  its  chromogenic  product  by O2without H2O2in the atmosphere and the concentration of O2was unfixed in real samples, creating uncertainty regarding

X. Liang, Prof. L. Han

College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

Qingdao Agricultural University

700Changcheng Road, Qingdao, Shandong266109, China E-mail: hanlei@qau.edu.cn

DOI: 10.1002/adfm.202001933

the  amount  of  oxidization. [16]   Moreover, the known nanozymes usually have interference colors, such as black (Fe3O4 [17]  and Co3O4), [18]   yellow  (CeO2 [19] and  V2O5), [20] red  (Au), [21] and  brown  (Pt, [22] MnO2, [15] and CuO), [10] which  can  disturb  the  judgment  of  color  and  even  the  absorption peak  for  colorimetric  assay.  To  solve  the above  problem,  we  developed  a  new  peroxidase-mimicking  nanozyme  with  white color and without oxidase-like activity.

Organophosphate  pesticides  (OPs)  are a class of highly effective, broad-spectrum toxicity insecticides, which can irreversibly inhibit  the  activity  of  acetylcholinesterase (AChE), causing neurotoxicity and leading to  the  death  of  crop  pests. [23] Similarly, OPs  in  the  environment  are  also  highly toxic  for  mammals. [24,25]   Among  the  various methods for analyzing OPs (such as chromatography-mass spectrometry, [26]

electrochemical sensing, [27] and fluorescent assay), [28]  the peroxidase-like nanozyme-based colorimetric assay demonstrates the great potential, because of its advantages of visualization, simplicity, and low cost. [29]  However, deoxidizing the actual sample measurement in the field is difficult. Therefore, developing the peroxidase-like  nanozymes  without  oxidase-like  activity  will be beneficial for the reliability of nanozyme-based OP detection.

As  white  non-transition  metal  oxides,  GeO2nanomaterials have attracted considerable attention in the application of chemical  catalysts,  optical  fibers,  and  semiconductors,  due  to  their excellent  chemical  activity,  superior  electrical  properties,  and low cost. [30]  However, only few reports exist on the use of GeO2nanomaterials  in  the  colorimetric  detection  of  OPs.  Although various  metal  oxide  nanozymes  have  been  synthesized,  the enzyme-like activity of GeO2has been rarely reported.

Herein, we first demonstrated that GeO2nanomaterials synthesized by hydrothermal approach possessed peroxidase-mimicking  activity.  GeO2nanozymes  are  not  only  white,  but  also lack  oxidase-mimicking activity;  therefore,  the  assay  system  is unaffected by O2interference and the colors of the nanozymes themselves  in  nanozyme-based  colorimetric  assay.  Interestingly,  compared  with  other  metal  oxides,  GeO2nanozymes were easily decomposed by thiocholine (TCh), AChE-catalyzed hydrolysate  of  acetylthiocholine  (ATCh),  which  enabled  their application  in  OP  detection.  As  a  typical  model  of  OPs,  paraoxon was chosen for the OP detection. The irreversible inhibition of AChE caused by paraoxon can reduce the production of TCh and maintain the enzyme-like activity of GeO2. The developed  GeO2nanozymes  and  the  proposed  strategy  could  open

www.advancedsciencenews.com an  interesting  avenue  for  ultrasensitive  and  reliable  analysis based on nanozymes without interferences of O2and color.


# 2. Results and Discussion



# 2.1. Preparation and Characterization of GeO2Nanozymes


We  began  our  investigation  with  the  hydrothermal  synthesis of  GeO2nanozymes.  First,  cetyltrimethylammonium  bromide (CTAB) was added as a surfactant into the basic solution of raw GeO2under  stirring  at  room  temperature.  Subsequently,  the mixture was incubated at100 ° C in a Teflon bottle. The product could  evenly  disperse  in  water.  After  vacuum  drying,  white powders were obtained ( Figure1a).

To  research  the  morphology  of  the  product,  transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed (Figure1b), and products showed besom-like morphology with uniform size (width of ≈ 100nm and length of ≈ 1 µ m). The high magnification TEM images exhibited that the 'head of besom' was composed of long strip with width of ≈ 10nm (Figure1c,d).  More morphology information of the products was revealed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Figure1e,f). Many long strips with  width  of ≈ 10nm  tidily  gathered  to  form3D  besom-like bundles, similar to that observed via TEM. In contrast, raw bulk GeO2exhibited  an  irregular  shape  and  large  size  (Figure  S1, Supporting Information).

To  further  compare  GeO2nanomaterials  and  raw  GeO2, small-angle  X-ray  diffraction  (XRD)  patterns  were  recorded (Figure1g).  For  GeO2nanomaterials,  four  broad  diffraction peaks  with  gradually  decreasing  intensities  were  observed, which  was  different  from  the  hexagonal,  cubic,  and  lamellar mesostructures, [31]   implying  an  intermediate  mesostructure. On  the  contrary,  no  diffraction  peaks  were  detected  for  raw GeO2, indicating the absence of an ordered mesostructure. The above results suggested that GeO2transformed into an ordered mesostructure during the synthesis.

To  evaluate  the  chemical  components  and  the  oxidation state  of  products,  X-ray  photoelectron  spectroscopy  (XPS)  was performed. The quantification table of elements (Table S1, Supporting  Information)  in  XPS  pattern  (Figure1h)  showed  that C1s accounted for52.36% of total elements, demonstrating the existence  of  CTAB.  The  high-resolution  Ge3d  XPS  spectrum showed the peak at31.61eV, which was typical value for Ge4 + , indicating  the  formation  of  GeO2 (Figure1i).  Meanwhile,  the XPS peak of O1s was observed at530.89eV, evidencing Ge  O bonds  (Figure1j).  The  above  results  proved  the  successful preparation of besom-like GeO2nanomaterials.


# 2.2. Peroxidase-Like Activity of GeO2Nanozymes without Oxidase-Like Activity


To  confirm  the  peroxidase-like  activity  of  as-prepared  GeO2nanomaterials, we separately mixed chromogenic substrates3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2,2-azinobis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic  acid)  (ABTS),  and o -phenylenediamine  (OPD)  with  the  acetate  buffer  containing  GeO2and H2O2; subsequently, blue, green, and yellow colors were observed

www.afm-journal.de after  the  reaction in the abovementioned solutions, respectively ( Figure2a). As shown in Figure2b, the GeO2nanozyme-catalyzed reaction solution exhibited a strong characteristic absorbance of oxidized TMB (TMB* + ) at650nm, whereas negligible absorbance could be observed without GeO2nanozymes. Similar results were observed  with  the  other  two  peroxidase  substrates  (ABTS  and OPD) (Figure2c,d). Considered together, the above results exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity and substrate universality of GeO2nanozymes, which were similar to those of natural peroxidase. Moreover, the absorbance of TMB* + increased as the concentration of GeO2nanozymes increased (Figure S2, Supporting Information),  conforming  the  intrinsic  peroxidase-like  activity of GeO2nanozymes. The peroxidase-mimicking activity of bulk GeO2was also researched and no obvious change was observed in the absorption peak, compared with the cases without GeO2 (Figure2b -d).  The  above  results  show  that  enzyme-mimicking activity is derived from nanostructured GeO2 .

To investigate the oxidase-like property of GeO2nanozymes, the reaction  without  H2O2was  conducted.  As  shown  in Figure2e, in air and even O2atmosphere, TMB could not be oxidized without H2O2under the catalysis of GeO2nanozymes, indicating  that  GeO2nanozymes  do  not  possess  oxidase-like activity.  Similar  results  were  also  obtained  by  using  ABTS and  OPD  as  substrates  (Figure2f,g),  confirming  that  GeO2nanozymes possess single peroxidase-like activity without oxidase-like  activity  and  that  peroxidase-mimicking  performance can be free from O2interference.

Moreover,  the  peroxidase-like  activity  of  GeO2nanozymes showed pH- and temperature-dependence, and the highest catalytic activity was achieved at pH4.0and50 ° C (Figure S3, Supporting Information), which was similar to natural peroxidase. Further,  the  pH  or  thermal  stability  of  GeO2nanozymes  was researched by incubating GeO2nanozymes at pH (1.0-11.0) or temperature (4-90 ° C) before the activity assay. For pH stability (Figure S4a, Supporting Information), GeO2nanozymes could maintain relative activity of above95% in the pH range of4.0-7 .0. For  thermal  stability  (Figure  S4b,  Supporting  Information), GeO2nanozymes could maintain relative activity of more than90% within50 ° C and still maintain about50% of the original activity at90 ° C. According to TEM characterization, the decline in  activity  may  be  derived  from  the  decomposition  of  GeO2nanozymes  after  incubation  (Figure  S5,  Supporting  Information).  As  the  relative  activity  of  GeO2nanozymes  was  almost100% at37 ° C, this temperature was chosen for the subsequent experiments.


# 2.3. Steady-State Kinetics Mechanism of GeO2Nanozymes


To evaluate the catalytic performance of GeO2nanozymes, the steady-state kinetics was investigated by independently changing the concentration of either H2O2 ( Figure3a) or TMB (Figure3b) and the data were in accordance with Michaelis-Menten equation. As a significance parameter of Michaelis-Menten equation, Km is inversely related to the affinity of substrate with enzyme. The Km  values  (0.42mm  for  TMB  and1.75mm  for  H2O2)  of GeO2nanozymes  were  lower  than  those  of  natural  enzymes, and  some  other  nanozymes  (Table  S2,  Supporting  Information), [1,32,33]   indicating that GeO2nanozymes exhibited a strong

www.advancedsciencenews.com

Figure1. Synthesis and characterizations of GeO2nanozymes. a) Synthesis schematic of GeO2nanozymes. b) TEM image of GeO2nanozymes. Enlarged view of the pane-marked areas of c) (b) and d) (c), respectively. e) SEM image of GeO2nanozymes. f) Enlarged view of the pane-marked areas of (e). g) Small-angle XRD patterns of GeO2nanozymes and raw GeO2 . h) XPS spectra of GeO2nanozymes. High-resolution XPS patterns of i) Ge3d and j) O1s.

affinity with the both substrates. On the other hand, as another key parameter for estimating the catalytic rate, the Vmax  value of GeO2nanozymes was23.4 × 10 -8M s -1 , which was higher than that of natural enzymes, [1]  and some other nanozymes (Table S2, Supporting  Information). [1,32-34]   Therefore,  GeO2nanozymes possessed an excellent affinity and catalytic rate, and would be an ideal candidate for peroxidase mimics.

To explore the kinetics mechanism of the catalytic reaction, Lineweaver-Burk plots were investigated (Figure3c,d). The two groups  of  lines  intersected  at  the  same  point,  indicating  that this is consistent with the sequence Bi-Bi mechanism. Therefore, both H2O2and TMB were first bound to GeO2nanozyme, and subsequently the products, TMB * + and H2O were released. For the1/ ν -1/[H2O2] plot (Figure3c), the intersection point was

Figure2. Peroxidase-like activity of GeO2nanozymes. a) Schematic of the catalytic activity of GeO2nanozymes. Typical absorption spectra of b) TMB, c) ABTS, and d) OPD oxidation catalyzed by GeO2nanozymes and controls in the presence of H2O2in the acetate buffer (pH4.0, 0.1m). Absorption spectra of catalytic oxidation of e) TMB, f) ABTS, and g) OPD by GeO2nanozymes under air, O2atmosphere or H2O2 .

slightly lower than the abscissa, showing that the apparent Km of H2O2was faintly increased with the increased concentration of  TMB.  For  the1/ ν -1/[TMB] plot,  the  intersection  point  was at the abscissa (Figure3d), indicating that the concentration of H2O2did  not  affect  the Km  value  of  TMB.  The  above  results suggest  that  during  the  catalytic  process,  GeO2nanozymes, TMB  and  H2O2formed  a  ternary  intermediate  complex  and the catalysis did not derive from the generation of free radicals. This was different with some enzymes and nanozymes, such as peroxidase, [1] Ag nanozymes, [35]  and Fe3O4nanozymes. [36]

To  confirm  the  above  kinetics  mechanism,  terephthalic acid  (TA)  was  selected  as  the  fluorescent  probe  for  tracking hydroxyl radicals ( · OH) to produce2-hydroxy terephthalic acid with an emission peak around425nm. As shown in Figure3e,

with  the  increased  dosages  of  GeO2nanozymes,  the  fluorescence  intensity  declined  at425nm,  implying  that,  not  only was · OH  not  formed  during  the  catalytic  process  but  also GeO2nanozyme  could  reduce  the · OH  generated  from  the self-decomposition of H2O2. On the other hand, O2 · --specific fluorescence probe, hydroethidine (HE) was used for tracking O2 · -to  produce  ethidium  with  an  emission  peak  around610nm.  As  shown  in  Figure3f,  no  fluorescence  emission peaks at610nm were observed, indicating that O2 · -was  not generated.  The  above  results  showed  that  GeO2nanozymes could consume H2O2without forming · OH and O2 · -.  That is to say, the peroxidase-like activity of GeO2nanozymes did not originate from free radicals, which conformed to the sequence Bi-Bi mechanism.

The Lineweaver-Burk plots for various concentrations of c) TMB (0.06, 0.09, and0.3mm) and d) H2O2 (0.3, 0.6, and1.2mm). # 2.4. Sensitive Activity Loss of GeO2Nanozymes with TCh


Interestingly,  GeO2nanozymes  showed  poor  stability  toward TCh, which was obtained by AChE-catalyzed hydrolysis ATCh. As shown in Figure4a, the characteristic absorption peak was clearly observed in the catalytic reaction system containing GeO2nanozymes, H2O2, and TMB. In sharp contrast, after the incubation of GeO2nanozymes with TCh, the absorbance peak was significantly reduced. The results show that TCh can decrease the catalytic activity of GeO2nanozymes. Further, the stability of GeO2nanozymes to TCh was compared with other metal oxide nanozymes containing V2O3 (Figure4b), Fe3O4 (Figure4c), and Co3O4 (Figure4d). After the incubation of the above nanozymes with TCh, the absorption peak slightly declined, demonstrating that  other  metal  oxide  nanozymes  were  less  sensitive  to  TCh than GeO2nanozymes (Figure4e). To study the reason for this, GeO2nanozymes,  following  their  incubation  with  TCh,  were

analyzed through TEM (Figure4f). The besom-like morphology of GeO2nanozymes had been decomposed by TCh and several scattered  fragments  was  observed.  The  above  results  showed that TCh could trigger the decomposition of GeO2nanozymes and efficiently retard the activity of GeO2nanozymes. So, GeO2nanozymes were considered to be used on the assay platform based on AChE-inhibition method.


# 2.5. Ultrasensitive Colorimetric OP Assay without Interferences of O2and Color


To  protect  humans,  environment,  and  food  production  from the harmful effects of OPs, it is of great significance to develop a simple and on-site assay strategy. As a candidate method, colorimetric  assay  has  been  extensively  applied  on  real  samples assay  because  of  its  simplicity,  flexibility,  and  visualization. [37]

www.advancedsciencenews.com

www.afm-journal.de

Figure4. Stability of GeO2nanozymes for TCh. The absorption spectra of reaction based on peroxidase-like activity of a) GeO2nanozymes, b) V2O3nanozymes, c) Fe3O4nanozymes, and d) Co3O4nanozymes before and after the incubation with TCh. e) The stability of various nanozymes to TCh. f) TEM image of GeO2nanozymes after incubation with TCh.

In addition, the utilization of nanozymes can reduce the utilization of enzymes and the cost of assay kits. Considering the low concentration of pesticide residues distributed in crops and environment, [37-39] it is urgently desirable to construct an ultrasensitive  colorimetric  assay  platform  based  on  nanozymes  for detecting ultra-trace OPs. AChE-inhibition methods have been widely  applied  on  the  detection  of  residual  OPs  in  food  and environment. Considering the TCh-sensitivity, non-oxidase-like activity, and whiteness of peroxidase-like GeO2nanozymes, we constructed  a  novel  colorimetric  OP  assay  platform  based  on the inhibition of AChE ( Figure5a). As a typical model of OPs, paraoxon was selected as the analyte and inhibitor of AChE. In the absence of paraoxon, active AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis  of  ATCh  into  TCh,  which  can  trigger  the  decomposition of  GeO2nanozymes,  resulting  in  the  loss  of  peroxidase-like activity.  In  the  presence  of  paraoxon,  AChE  was  irreversibly inactivated  and  TCh  production  was  suppressed.  Therefore,

OPs can indirectly  affect  the  peroxidase-mimicking  activity  of GeO2nanozyme;  the  colorimetric  assay  based  on  H2O2and TMB  can  indirectly  detect  pesticide  concentration.  Owing  to the  white  color  of  GeO2nanozymes, the blue color of TMB* + can be readily distinguished. As GeO2nanozymes did not have oxidase-like activity, the assay remained unaffected by O2concentration during on-site detection.

To explore the feasibility of the above assay strategy, the paraoxon assay was performed both in air and in O2atmosphere. As shown in Figure5b, a weak absorbance peak was observed at650nm in the system without paraoxon; however, the absorbance peak significantly increased in the presence of paraoxon, confirming  that  paraoxon  could  give  rise  to  positively  related optical signal in a system based on GeO2nanozymes. Moreover, while neither ATCh nor AChE could alone impair the catalytic activity of GeO2nanozymes alone, the coexistence of ATCh and AChE  could  impair  that,  thus  notarizing  the  TCh-sensitivity

www.advancedsciencenews.com

www.afm-journal.de

Figure5. Colorimetric paraoxon detection based on GeO2nanozymes. a) Schematic illustration of colorimetric sensing strategy. b) Feasibility analysis of paraoxon detection method. c) Photograph of the assay system containing various concentration of paraoxon. d) Corresponding absorption spectra. e) Working curve for paraoxon detection.

of GeO2nanozymes. Interestingly, the absorption peaks of the assay system with paraoxon in air or in O2atmosphere almost overlapped, conforming that the absence of oxidase-like activity made the proposed assay strategy free from disturbance of O2 .

Besides  the  pesticide  assay  proposed  in  this  work,  many nanozyme-based  assay  systems  also  rely  on  the  detection  of H2O2, such as the glucose assay. [40,41]   Considering the oxidaselike activity could distort the results of the H2O2detection. To prove  universality  of  the  proposed  strategy  and  highlight  the superiority of as-prepared GeO2nanozymes, glucose detection was performed by coupling glucose oxidation catalyzed by glucose oxidase and H2O2detection catalyzed by GeO2nanozymes. Regardless  of  whether  the  reaction  solutions  were  deoxidized after  glucose  oxidation,  the  colors  of  the  solutions  were  consistent  (Figure  S6,  Supporting  Information).  As  the  oxidation of different  amounts  of  glucose  consumes corresponding

amounts of O2, and the concentration of O2in real samples is constantly changed, [16]   GeO2nanozymes can not only enhance reliability in the presence of O2but also eliminate deoxidation process for the corresponding assay strategy.

Under the  proposed  strategy,  the  blue  color  of  the  solution gradually  deepened  with  an  increase  in  paraoxon  (Figure5c). Notably,  GeO2nanozymes  did  not  have  an  interference  color, which is convenient for the naked eye to judge the color of the reaction  solution.  For  the  quantitative  assay  of  paraoxon,  the absorbance  spectra  of  the  reaction  solution  were  measured and the maximal absorption peak at650nm gradually intensified with an increase in paraoxon (Figure5d). Then, the standard working  curve  was  plotted,  and  the  regression  equation  was y = 0.0067x + 0.014 ( R2 = 0.996) with the linear range of0.1-50pm (Figure5e). Encouragingly, the proposed method showed ultralow limit of detection (LOD, 14fm), which was1-5orders

of  magnitude lower than those of other optical methods (fluorescence [28,42-44] and spectrophotometry), [29,45-47]   and even lower than or similar with some electrochemical methods (Table S3, Supporting  Information). [48-52] The  ultrahigh  sensitivity  may derive from the high TCh-sensitivity, interference-free color, and non-oxidase-like activity of peroxidase-like GeO2nanozymes.


# 2.6. OP Analysis in Real Samples


To estimate the applicability and reliability of this assay strategy, two types of environmental waters (tap water and river water) and two types of food juices (apple juice and cucumber juice) were used as test samples ( Table1 ). Notably, the color interference  of  samples  could  be  avoided  because  the  residual  GeO2nanozymes  were  collected  by  centrifugation  after  incubating with real samples. Owing to the ultralow LOD of the proposed method,  the  samples  could  be  diluted  to  avoid  the  interference of antioxidants. The concentration values were calculated according  to  the  working  curve  (Figure5e).  The  standardaddition  method  was  applied  and  the  recoveries  of  paraoxon were  within97 .3-103.5%,  showing  high  reliability  and  accuracy.  The  relative  standard  deviation  (RSD)  was  within4.3%, suggesting the excellent reproducibility and precision.


# 3. Conclusion


Herein,  a  white  nanozyme,  a  besom-like  GeO2nanomaterial,  was  discovered.  After  the  activity  assay,  GeO2nanozymes were  found  to  possess  only  peroxidase-like  activity  and  not oxidase-like  activity.  Moreover,  GeO2nanozymes  also  exhibited  excellent  affinity  and  catalytic  rate,  and  the  catalysis  did not derive from the generation of free radicals, which was different from some enzymes and nanozymes. Interestingly, GeO2nanozymes showed poor stability to TCh, compared with some other nanozymes.  Because  of  TCh-sensitivity,  non-oxidaselike  activity  and  whiteness  of  GeO2nanozymes,  the  proposed assay method eliminated the disturbance of O2and nanozymes' colors, exhibiting an ultrahigh sensitivity and a low LOD. The as-synthesized  GeO2nanozymes  would  be  an  ideal  candidate for  peroxidase  mimics.  This  detection  method  would  provide

an  effective  solution  for  overcoming  the  oxidase-like  activity and color of many peroxidase-like nanozymes.


# 4. Experimental Section


Chemicals  and  Materials :  H2O2  (30%),  OPD,  ABTS,  and  TMB  were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). AChE, HE, TA,  sodium  hydroxide  (NaOH),  and  CTAB  were  purchased  from  SigmaAldrich  (USA).  GeO2were  purchased  by  Alfa  Aesar  (China).  Paraoxon were purchased from Huaerbo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). ATCh iodide were purchased by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The ultrapure water was obtained by Milli-Q system (18.2M Ω cm).

Apparatus :  The  morphology of GeO2nanozymes was characterized by TEM H-7650 (Hitachi, Japan) and SEM S-4800 (Hitachi, Japan). Small-angle XRD patterns were recorded on X-ray diffractometer D8Advance (Bruker, Germany). To study the elementary composition and valence state of GeO2nanozymes,  the  XPS  measurement  was  conducted  on  a  spectrometer ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The absorption spectra and the time-dependent absorbance were recorded on a microplate reader SpectraMax  M2e  (Molecular  Devices,  USA).  The  fluorescence  emission was obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan).

Synthesis of GeO2Nanozymes : The hydrothermal strategy was applied to synthesis GeO2nanozymes. In a typical procedure, GeO2 (0.9m) and NaOH solution (0.3m) were added into10mL of water and stirred at25 ° C overnight. Then, 12mL of CTAB solution (0.3m) was poured into the above mixed solution. After stirring for another6h, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon bottle and heated at100 ° C for56h. Subsequently, the impurities were removed from the resulting product by washing with ultrapure  water  and  alcohol,  respectively.  Last,  the  as-prepared  GeO2nanozymes were obtained via the vacuum drying at room temperature.

Peroxidase-Like Activity Assay  of  GeO2Nanozymes : Typically, the peroxidase-like activity of GeO2nanozymes was investigated by catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. The acetate buffer solution (0.1m,  pH4.0)  containing  H2O2 (1.2mm),  TMB (0.6mm), and GeO2nanozymes (0.1mg mL -1 ) was incubated at37 ° C for30min. And then, the  characteristic  absorbance  of  oxidized  TMB  (TMB* + )  at650nm ( ε 650nm = 39 000m -1cm -1 ) was detected. The peroxidase-like activity of GeO2nanozymes was also investigated by replacing TMB with ABTS and OPD in the same condition. Each activity assay experiment was repeated at  least  three  times.  The  effect  of  pH  and  temperature  on  peroxidasemimicking activity of GeO2nanozymes were also explored by the above method, except for changing pH (pH1.0-11.0) or temperature (4-90 ° C).

Stability  of  GeO2Nanozymes :  The  pH  and  thermal  stability  of  GeO2nanozymes were measured by the above assay after GeO2nanozymes were incubated at different pH (pH1.0-12.0) or in various temperatures (4-90 ° C) for an hour.

For  the  stability  of  GeO2nanozymes  to  TCh,  various  nanozymes (GeO2, V2O3, Fe3O4 , and Co3O4) were investigated. Typically, the phosphate  buffer  (PB,  0.1m,  pH8.0)  containing  AChE  (20 µ g  mL -1 ) and ATCh (10mm) was incubated at37 ° C for30min, and then GeO2nanozymes  (0.1mg  mL -1 )  were  added  into  the  mixture.  The  given concentrations were final concentrations. After the incubation for20min, the residual GeO2nanozymes was collected by centrifugation, and  employed  to  the  above  activity  assay.  For  the  comparison  with other  nanozymes,V2O3nanozymes  were  synthesized  by  calcination. [53] Co3O4nanozymes  were  synthesized  by  hydrothermal  process, [8]   and Fe3O4nanozymes  were  synthesized  by  the  co-precipitation  method. [1] Subsequently, these nanozymes were used to replace GeO2nanozymes for the above assay at the same condition.

Steady-State  Kinetics  Assay :  The  steady-state  kinetics  analysis  of  the peroxidase-like activity of GeO2nanozymes was conducted by changing the concentrations (0-1mm) of TMB at the constant H2O2concentration (4mm)  or  varying  the  concentration  (0-4mm)  of  H2O2at  the  fixed concentration  of  TMB  (1mm).  For  obtaining  the  apparent  kinetic parameters ( Km and Vmax ), the data were fitting by the Michaelis-Menten

www.advancedsciencenews.com equation: ν = Vmax × [S]/( Km + [S]),  where ν is  the  initial  velocity, Vmax represents the maximal reaction velocity, [S] represents the concentration of substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant.

To further explore the kinetics mechanism, the above peroxidase-like activity assay was conducted, except for changing the concentrations of H2O2 (0.3, 0.6, and1.2mm) or TMB (0.06, 0.09, and0.3mm) one at a time.  The  kinetic  data  were  plotted  according  to  the  Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal curves.

Oxidase-Like  Activity  Assay  of  GeO2Nanozymes :  For  the  oxidase-like activity  of  GeO2nanozymes, the reaction system was similar with that of the peroxidase-like activity, except that H2O2was not added and the reaction was conducted under different atmospheres (air and O2 ) for30min.

Colorimetric Assay of OPs : Typically, 100 µ L of different concentrations of  paraoxon  (0,  0.1,  2,  5,  10,  15,  30,  50,  70,  100pm)  were  mixed  with20 µ L  of  PB  solutions  (0.1m,  pH8.0)  containing  AChE  (10 µ g  mL -1 ). After the incubation for20min at37 ° C, 20 µ L of ATCh solution (10mm) and20 µ L of GeO2nanozymes solution (1mg mL -1 ) were added into the above mixture respectively for another20min incubation. The residual GeO2nanozymes was collected by centrifugation, and added into200 µ L of  acetate  buffer  (pH4.0,  0.1m)  containing  TMB  (0.6mm)  and  H2O2 (1.2mm). Finally,  the  absorbance  of  the  above  reaction  was  measured after30min.  Each  experiment  was  repeated  three  times.  The  LOD was calculated by the equation LOD = (3 σ / s ),  where σ is  the  standard deviation of blank signals and s is the slope of the calibration curve.

OP  Detection  in  Real  Samples :  The  applicability  of  the  proposed method was researched by using environmental waters (tap water and river water) and food juices (apple juice and cucumber juice) as the real samples.  The  recovery  was  investigated  by  standard-addition  method. Tap  water  was  collected  from  the  authors'  laboratory,  river  water  was collected  from  a  local  river.  Food  juices  were  obtained  by  juicing  the apple  and  cucumber  in  PB  (0.1m,  pH8.0)  purchased  from  the  local market.  After  injection  of  paraoxon  (10and50pm)  into  the  above samples,  they  were  filtered  through0.22 µ m  membranes  to  remove insoluble  impurity  and  diluted  to  appropriate  concentration  with  PB (0.1m, pH8.0). Subsequently, the sample solutions were detected by the proposed colorimetric assay based on GeO2nanozymes. The experiment of each sample was independently tested for five times.
